subject: [RFC PATCH 34/40] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary: Reviewer David Sterba agreed that GFP flags would be improved and more self-documenting in the functions where they are called, but noted that this patch is not standalone and suggested cleaning it up later to allow the series to proceed.
reviews.raw_body:
Agreed, the GFP flags would be better and also a bit more self
documenting where the functions are called. The patch is not standalone
so I don't mind having the booleans, we can clean it up later to let the
40-patch series proceed.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author agrees that the patch could be submitted independently and is willing to rework it by passing GFP masks from each caller, planning to send a revised version soon.
reviews.raw_body:
I think this patch could go in by itself. It doesn't
depend on anything else in the series.

I sent it with this series mostly because it's needed
to make the series work, and to provide context on why
it's needed.

I'm happy to resend it with a GFP mask passed in by
each caller. That would look better, indeed!

I'll send you guys a cleaned up patch this coming week.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-05-23.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system to allocate extent buffer pages attached to btree_inode as movable, allowing them to be migrated and reducing fragmentation in the page-superblock allocator. The change targets a dominant source of fragmentation observed on large test systems, where ~28 GB of btree_inode pages were parked across many tainted superpageblocks, preventing 1 GiB hugepage allocation from those regions. By adding the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to one allocation site and passing it through to other functions, the patch enables these pages to be moved to movable pageblocks, where they can be drained by the background defragger without needing a fallback LRU-sample approach.

reviews.author: Matthew Wilcox
reviews.summary: Reviewer Matthew Wilcox expressed concern that the patch's use of GFP_NOFS is a code smell, suggesting it should be replaced with memalloc_nofs_save/restore calls instead. He recommended lifting the GFP_NOFS to the callers and passing in GFP_NOFS | __GFP_MOVABLE (or plain GFP_NOFS) for now, allowing for future changes to GFP_KERNEL where warranted.
reviews.raw_body:
If you're respinning anyway ...

Every use of GFP_NOFS is a bad code smell.  Filesystems should be using
memalloc_nofs_save/restore.  The problem is that it takes deep filesystem
knowledge to understand where the memalloc_nofs_save() should start.
It would be good to lift the GFP_NOFS to the callers of this function as
it gets us slightly closer to being able to replace it with GFP_KERNEL
in the places it's not actually needed.

ie what I'm asking for is, please don't do the 'extra_gfp' thing.
Just pass in gfp from the callers and make them all pass in GFP_NOFS |
__GFP_MOVABLE (or a plain GFP_NOFS) for now.  Someone can come along
later and change them to GFP_KERNEL later where warranted.

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Reviewer suggested that the patch could be improved by bubbling up the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to a higher layer, which would make it more useful and reduce the need for manual intervention.
reviews.raw_body:
Note that is should be pretty trivial to just bubble it up a layer
for changes like this.  This will naturally gravitate to more useful
places.  To make it fully correct it will need manual intervention,
but generally bubbling up should improve the situation.

Same for GFP_NOIO.

The change targets a dominant source of fragmentation observed on large test systems, where ~28 GB of btree_inode pages were parked across many tainted superpageblocks, preventing 1 GiB hugepage allocation from those regions. By adding the __GFP_MOVABLE flag to one allocation site and passing it through to other functions, the patch enables these pages to be moved to movable pageblocks, where they can be drained by the background defragger without needing a fallback LRU-sample approach.
